rows.Question: You are in the BB at 100bb. The BTN opens. You hold AA. Best action?
rows.Explanation: AA: 3-bet for value vs BTN open — strong enough to build the pot OOP and 4-bet/stack-off vs many. Vs opponents who over-fold to 3-bets: add more bluff 3-bets. Vs call-happy openers: cut bluffs, 3-bet value only and value-bet thinner postflop.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: You are in the BB at 100bb. The BTN opens. You hold QQ. Best action?
rows.Explanation: QQ: 3-bet for value vs BTN open — strong enough to build the pot OOP and 4-bet/stack-off vs many. Vs opponents who over-fold to 3-bets: add more bluff 3-bets. Vs call-happy openers: cut bluffs, 3-bet value only and value-bet thinner postflop.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: You are in the BB at 100bb. The BTN opens. You hold TT. Best action?

rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: You are in the BB at 100bb. The BTN opens. You hold 88. Best action?
rows.Explanation: 88: flat to set-mine and realize showdown vs BTN; the BB price is too good to fold, too thin to 3-bet for value. Vs big sizings/short stacks set-mining value drops — fold small pairs more; vs deep loose opens, flat freely.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: You are in the BB at 100bb. The BTN opens. You hold AJs. Best action?

rows.Question: You are in the BB at 100bb. The BTN opens. You hold AQo. Best action?

rows.Explanation: Q7o: below the BB-defend threshold vs CO at 100bb — the worst of the price-driven range, fold. Vs tiny opens or very loose openers this enters the defend range.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: You are in the BB at 100bb. The HJ opens. You hold AA. Best action?
rows.Explanation: AA: 3-bet for value vs HJ open — strong enough to build the pot OOP and 4-bet/stack-off vs many. Vs opponents who over-fold to 3-bets: add more bluff 3-bets. Vs call-happy openers: cut bluffs, 3-bet value only and value-bet thinner postflop.

rows.Question: You are in the BB at 100bb. The HJ opens. You hold 88. Best action?

rows.Explanation: J8s: 3-bet bluff vs SB — blocker + suited playability; folds out better hands, has equity when called. Vs opponents who over-fold to 3-bets: add more bluff 3-bets. Vs call-happy openers: cut bluffs, 3-bet value only and value-bet thinner postflop.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: You are in the BB at 100bb. The SB opens. You hold JTo. Best action?

rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Effective 5bb, folded to you in the UTG with AA. Push/fold decision?
rows.Explanation: AA: first-in jam from UTG at 5bb — pairs jam well short — strong equity when called plus fold equity. Villain calls roughly ~9-12%.

rows.Explanation: AQo: Short-stacked, you must manufacture chips; re-shove over an open captures blinds/antes through fold equity. Only the winner's premium remains — play near chipev — jam the higher-equity end and skip the marginal calls.

rows.Explanation: AKs: As chip leader you carry the lowest bubble factor — re-shove over an open pressures covered mediums who must fold (seats are flat-paid — chips above the lock threshold are nearly worthless). Low personal risk, high fold equity: accumulate while others play scared.
rows.SourceSpot: ICM/Satellite Bubble — Re-shove ov/Big stack / chip leader/AKs
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Satellite Bubble — Re-shove over an open. You are the big stack / chip leader (12 left, 10 seats) in the SB with A9s. Correct play?
rows.Explanation: A9s: As chip leader you carry the lowest bubble factor — re-shove over an open pressures covered mediums who must fold (seats are flat-paid — chips above the lock threshold are nearly worthless). Low personal risk, high fold equity: accumulate while others play scared.
rows.SourceSpot: ICM/Satellite Bubble — Re-shove ov/Big stack / chip leader/A9s
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Satellite Bubble — Re-shove over an open. You are the big stack / chip leader (12 left, 10 seats) in the SB with KJs. Correct play?
rows.Explanation: KJs: As chip leader you carry the lowest bubble factor — re-shove over an open pressures covered mediums who must fold (seats are flat-paid — chips above the lock threshold are nearly worthless). Low personal risk, high fold equity: accumulate while others play scared.
rows.SourceSpot: ICM/Satellite Bubble — Re-shove ov/Big stack / chip leader/KJs
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Satellite Bubble — Re-shove over an open. You are the big stack / chip leader (12 left, 10 seats) in the SB with 76s. Correct play?
rows.Explanation: 76s: Under extreme ICM pressure (seats are flat-paid — chips above the lock threshold are nearly worthless) this hand sits below the risk-premium-adjusted threshold — fold and protect tournament $EV.
rows.SourceSpot: ICM/Satellite Bubble — Re-shove ov/Big stack / chip leader/76s
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Satellite Bubble — Re-shove over an open. You are the big stack / chip leader (12 left, 10 seats) in the SB with ATo. Correct play?
rows.Explanation: ATo: As chip leader you carry the lowest bubble factor — re-shove over an open pressures covered mediums who must fold (seats are flat-paid — chips above the lock threshold are nearly worthless). Low personal risk, high fold equity: accumulate while others play scared.
rows.SourceSpot: ICM/Satellite Bubble — Re-shove ov/Big stack / chip leader/ATo
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: A tight reg only calls flop and turn, then leads big on a blank river. His range is most likely:
rows.Explanation: Passive flat-flat lines cap a range; a sudden big river lead from a capped range is usually a missed draw bluffing or a slowplayed nut — read the player, but the line itself screams polarization on a blank.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: You hold the A♠ on a four-flush board (spades). This blocker means villain:
rows.Explanation: Holding the ace of the flush suit removes nut-flush combos from villain's range, making your bluffs more credible and their value-raises less likely.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Villain checks back the flop in a single-raised pot. On the turn his range is usually:
rows.Explanation: Most strong hands bet the flop for value/protection; checking back caps the range toward medium-strength and give-ups, so turn probe bets print.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Counting combos: on a board with no pair/flush, villain repped a set. Sets available are 3 combos each, but two-pair is 6+. This tells you:
rows.OptionA: Value-raises are combinatorially rarer than you fear
rows.Explanation: Sets are only 3 combos each; players overestimate how often villain 'has the set'. Combinatorics shows value is rarer than intuition suggests, supporting more hero-calls/bluffs.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: A recreational player snap-calls flop and turn then tanks on the river before calling. This usually means:
rows.Explanation: Snap-calls are often draws or one-pair; a river tank-call is typically a marginal bluff-catcher deciding whether to pay off — value-bet thinner against this pattern.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: On A♥K♥2♣, the preflop raiser (IP) has range advantage because:
rows.Explanation: High, dry boards favor the preflop aggressor's condensed strong range; this justifies a high-frequency small c-bet.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Villain 3-bets preflop, then checks back a low flop like 7♦6♦4♣. Their range is likely:
rows.OptionD: Capped — big pairs/AK would often c-bet
rows.Explanation: A 3-bettor who checks a low connected flop usually caps toward AK/AQ giving up and unpaired big cards, so you can attack turns aggressively.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Which hand makes the BEST river bluff: it should block villain's:
rows.Explanation: Effective bluffs block the hands villain continues with (value); blocking their folds is an anti-blocker and makes the bluff worse.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Villain's preflop flat then flop check-raise on a wet board most credibly represents:
rows.Explanation: Check-raises on wet boards are polar: strong made hands plus semi-bluff draws; bluff-catchers should consider equity and blockers before continuing.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: A player who never folds (station) on the river should be played by:
rows.Explanation: Against a calling station, expand thin value and cut bluffs — they pay off worse hands and won't fold to pressure.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: You bet, villain raises small on the river. A tiny raise from a non-tricky player usually means:
rows.Explanation: Small river raises from straightforward players are usually value seeking calls; without a strong bluff-catcher, fold marginal hands.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: On a paired board (KK4), the player with more trips/full houses (nut advantage) is usually:
rows.Explanation: The aggressor holds more Kx in their condensed range on most paired high boards, giving nut advantage and licensing larger/overbet sizings.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: If villain's line is consistent with ONLY draws and you hold a blocker to the nut draw, you should:
rows.Explanation: Blocking the nut draw reduces villain's strongest made/draw combos, increasing the chance their bet is a weaker draw or air — proceed more aggressively.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Multiway, a player bets into two opponents on a dry flop. This usually indicates:
rows.Explanation: Betting into multiple players multiway is rarely a bluff; ranges are value-weighted, so respect the bet and tighten continues.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Villain over-folds turns after calling flop. The exploit:
rows.Explanation: If a player calls flop too wide but folds turns, increasing your turn double-barrel bluff frequency captures the fold equity they surrender.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: At microstakes cash, the most reliable population exploit is:
rows.OptionA: Value-bet thinner and bluff less (they over-call)
rows.Explanation: Micro pools are inelastic callers; thin value prints while bluffs get paid off — cut bluffs, widen value.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Live $1/$2-$2/$5 players most commonly under-do which action?
rows.Explanation: Live pools flat far too often and 3-bet rarely; you can over-fold to their rare 3-bets and steal/iso relentlessly.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Vs a tight-passive online reg pool on the river, you should:
rows.OptionC: Respect big bets, fold marginal bluff-catchers
rows.Explanation: Tight-passive regs under-bluff rivers; their big bets are value-weighted, so fold more bluff-catchers than GTO.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Recreational players' biggest preflop leak you can exploit:
rows.OptionD: Calling too wide out of position
rows.Explanation: Recs over-call OOP with dominated hands; isolate wide and value-bet relentlessly postflop.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: In low-buy-in online MTTs, players near the bubble tend to:
rows.Explanation: Low-stakes fields play scared on bubbles; a big stack can widen steals/3-bet-jams to harvest chips.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Home-game opponents often:
rows.OptionB: Overvalue top pair and pay off big bets
rows.Explanation: Casual home games are loose-passive and call down with weak top pairs; size up for value and bluff sparingly.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Club-game regulars who know each other tend to:
rows.OptionC: Play too passively / avoid big confrontations (collusion-averse, social)
rows.Explanation: Social club dynamics often reduce aggression; exploit by stealing pots and value-betting, but stay alert to soft-collusion.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Vs a maniac who bets every street, the counter is:
rows.OptionD: Bluff-catch wider, let them barrel into your strength
rows.Explanation: Against over-aggression, tighten your betting and call down with more bluff-catchers since their range is bluff-heavy.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Mid-stakes online cash pools are best exploited by:
rows.OptionA: Small, population-read deviations off a GTO base
rows.Explanation: Mid-stakes regs are competent; edges come from small, targeted deviations on specific nodes, not wild exploits.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: High-buy-in MTT players differ in that you should:
rows.OptionB: Stay close to GTO; exploits are smaller and reciprocal
rows.Explanation: Tougher fields punish imbalance; lean on solid GTO with only small, well-supported deviations.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Satellite-tournament fields near the bubble:
rows.OptionC: Refuse to call off (extreme survival) — steal relentlessly
rows.Explanation: Satellite players cling to seats; with chips above the lock you face almost no calls — apply maximal fold-equity pressure.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Sit-and-go players commonly leak by:
rows.OptionD: Playing too tight in the mid-game, too loose on the bubble
rows.Explanation: SNG regs often misjudge mid-game aggression and bubble ICM; exploit by accumulating mid-game and pressuring the bubble.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Vs a pool that c-bets 100% of flops, you should:
rows.OptionA: Float wider IP and check-raise more OOP
rows.Explanation: Auto-c-betting pools are exploited by attacking the high-frequency, low-equity portion of their betting range.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: A pool that never folds to 3-bets requires you to:
rows.Explanation: If 3-bet bluffs never fold out worse hands, switch to a value-heavy (linear) 3-bet range and bluff less.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Against opponents who fold too much to flop raises, the exploit is:
rows.Explanation: Over-folding to raises means your fold equity is high; expand your raising (including bluffs) to print EV.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Antes are posted. Compared to no antes, your opening ranges should:
rows.Explanation: Antes add dead money, improving the reward for taking the pot uncontested — open and steal wider across positions.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: At 12bb effective in the CO, your primary preflop tool is:
rows.Explanation: Around 10-12bb, raise-folding is inefficient; jam-or-fold captures fold equity and avoids tough postflop spots.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: With 28bb you face a 2.2x open from the CO in the SB with A♠T♠. Best tool:
rows.OptionB: 3-bet (can call a 4-bet or 3-bet/fold with a plan)
rows.Explanation: At ~25-30bb you regain a raise/3-bet structure; ATs is a strong 3-bet with playability rather than a jam or a weak OOP flat.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Early in a tournament (100bb+), you should mostly:
rows.OptionC: Play like deep cash; build chips, avoid ICM-thinking
rows.Explanation: ICM is nearly irrelevant deep and early; apply cash fundamentals and accumulate without unnecessary survival mode.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: A short stack's main source of EV preflop is:
rows.Explanation: Short stacks profit by shoving for fold equity (blinds/antes) plus equity when called — pick high-fold-equity spots.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Reshoving (jamming over an open) is generally higher-skill than first-in jamming because:
rows.OptionA: You must account for the opener's range and your blockers
rows.Explanation: Reshove ranges depend on the open size/position and use blockers; they're more nuanced than nested first-in Nash jams.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: As the big stack at a final table, you should target:
rows.Explanation: Mediums fear busting before pay jumps; pressuring them is cheap and high-fold-equity, unlike attacking shorts (low FE) or bigs (risky).
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: On a satellite bubble, holding a seat-locking stack, A-A is:
rows.OptionC: Often a FOLD if calling risks your locked seat
rows.Explanation: When chips above the lock are nearly worthless, even aces can be a fold because busting forfeits a guaranteed seat.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: PKO (bounty) tournaments shift your calling ranges to be:
rows.OptionD: Wider when you cover (the bounty adds value)
rows.Explanation: Covering a shorter stack lets you win their bounty, adding immediate EV that justifies calling somewhat wider than chipEV.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: A medium stack on the money bubble facing a big-stack jam should:
rows.Explanation: High bubble factor vs the cover means the risk premium turns marginal chipEV calls into folds — preserve $EV.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Open-sizing in the mid-stage of an MTT (with antes) is typically:
rows.Explanation: Antes and ICM favor efficient small opens that risk fewer chips while still attacking the dead money.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Future-game (ICM lookahead) means:
rows.OptionC: Considering upcoming pay jumps, not just the current one
rows.Explanation: Strong ICM play weighs the ladder of pay jumps ahead; a marginal call risks future laddering, not only the immediate jump.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: With 15bb in the BB facing a min-raise, a strong tool is:
rows.Explanation: At ~15bb the BB can reshove over a min-raise leveraging fold equity, prioritizing hands with blockers and equity when called.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Deep at a final table, the biggest mistake a medium stack makes is:
rows.OptionA: Calling off against the chip leader
rows.Explanation: Mediums leak the most by flipping against the cover; ICM says avoid the big stack and pressure other mediums/shorts instead.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: A big stack's bubble strategy should be:
rows.Explanation: The big stack has the lowest bubble factor and should attack covered mediums constantly while risk is low and fold equity high.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: 'Resulting' is the error of:
rows.OptionC: Judging a decision by its outcome, not its quality
rows.Explanation: Poker is high-variance; good decisions often lose short-term. Evaluate process (decision quality at the time), not the runout.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: The most reliable in-the-moment tilt intervention is:
rows.Explanation: A pre-committed stop-loss and breaks protect your A-game; trying to 'win it back' while tilted compounds losses.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: During a downswing, the healthiest reframe is:
rows.OptionA: It's expected variance over a finite sample
rows.Explanation: Understanding standard deviation reframes downswings as normal noise, protecting confidence and decision quality.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Proper cash bankroll management is typically:
rows.Explanation: Adequate buy-ins ensure variance doesn't bust you, letting a real edge realize over the long run.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Playing too many tables usually harms:
rows.Explanation: Beyond your focus capacity, error rate rises; fewer tables at higher quality often beats more tables on autopilot.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: A pre-session warm-up primarily helps by:
rows.OptionD: Loading key concepts and getting into your A-game
rows.Explanation: Warm-ups prime decision-making and reduce early-session autopilot mistakes.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Revenge-spewing at one opponent is a symptom of:
rows.Explanation: Letting one player provoke you abandons range-based logic; keep decisions impersonal and EV-driven.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Goal-setting in poker should focus on:
rows.OptionB: Process goals (study, decisions) over results goals
rows.Explanation: You control process, not short-term results; process goals drive improvement and reduce outcome-tilt.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: The clearest sign you should quit a session is:
rows.OptionC: Noticing tilt cues or fatigue affecting decisions
rows.Explanation: Self-awareness of degraded decision quality (tilt/fatigue) is the cue to stop, regardless of whether you're up or down.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Confidence in poker is best built on:
rows.OptionD: A track record of good decisions and solid study
rows.Explanation: Sustainable confidence comes from process and preparation, not volatile results that variance distorts.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Variance means that over a small sample, results:
rows.OptionA: Can diverge greatly from your true win rate
rows.Explanation: Short-run results are dominated by variance; large samples are needed before results reflect true skill.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Discipline at the tables most often means:
rows.OptionB: Folding/quitting when correct, even when bored
rows.Explanation: Discipline is doing the +EV thing (tight folds, game selection, quitting) despite boredom or the urge for action.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: After a big loss, the highest-EV action is usually to:
rows.OptionC: Take a break / drop stakes, not chase
rows.Explanation: Chasing losses while emotional lowers decision quality; stepping back or dropping down protects your bankroll and game.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Effective session reviews focus on:
rows.OptionD: Decision quality at each node, cards hidden first
rows.Explanation: Reviewing decisions (ideally judging before seeing the runout) trains process-thinking and removes resulting bias.
rows.Author: Tay

rows.Question: Game selection is a mental-game/meta skill because:
rows.OptionA: Ego resists table-selecting; discipline here is pure profit
rows.Explanation: Choosing softer games multiplies edge more than any solver line; overcoming ego to leave tough games is a discipline skill.
rows.Author: Tay
